title: Chapter 15: Cordelia's Ledger
content: The second time I walked backward into memory, I didn't fall.

That's the thing nobody tells you about magic — the first time is always disaster. Stumbling, gasping, retching up your own heartbeat like a fish on a dock. But the second time? The second time, your body knows the way. Your bones remember the shape of the door.
I closed my eyes in the cottage, and when I opened them, I was standing in Cordelia Buena's kitchen, and the year was 1798.

The smell hit me first. Beeswax. Thick, golden, saturating the air like a varnish on the world. Someone had polished every surface in this house within an inch of its life — the wide pine floorboards, the deal table, the ladder-back chairs, the mantel over the hearth where a clock ticked with metronomic cruelty. Beeswax and something else underneath it. Something sour and patient. The smell of betrayal, I realized. The smell of someone polishing a lie until it shone.

The farmhouse was neat. Devastatingly, obsessively neat. Every pot on its hook. Every jar on its shelf, labels facing forward like soldiers at inspection. The herbs hanging from the ceiling rafters were bundled with identical twine, spaced exactly evenly. The hearth was swept clean — not a speck of ash, not a smudge of soot. The window glass had been wiped so thoroughly that the autumn light fell through in golden sheets, pooling on the floor in amber rectangles that looked, to my eyes, like pages of legal documents.
Every object was accounted for. Every surface was clean.
Everything was wrong.

I knew this smell. I knew this house. Not because I'd been here before — I hadn't — but because I'd lived in its descendants. The Buena women kept house like this when they were keeping secrets. When my grandmother was hiding her hex work from my grandfather, she'd scrubbed the kitchen until the linoleum wore through. When my mother was concealing how bad the money had gotten, she'd organized the pantry by alphabet and color. Cleanliness as camouflage. Order as obfuscation. A house this tidy wasn't well-kept — it was armored.

I moved through the kitchen on soft feet, though I needn't have bothered. I was a ghost here, or something like one — present but untouchable, there but then. The floorboards didn't creak under my weight. The air didn't shift around my passing. I was light and memory and nothing more.
The farmhouse opened into a narrow hallway, and the hallway led to a study.
She was there.

Cordelia Buena sat at a desk by the window, and the amber light fell across her like a benediction, turning her profile to copper and her hair to dark silk. She was younger than I'd expected — or younger than I'd imagined, which isn't the same thing. Forty, perhaps. The lines at the corners of her eyes were fine, like the scratches a cat leaves on a door it wants opened. Her hands were ink-stained. Her spine was straight. Her face was the face of a woman who has just discovered something terrible and is refusing, with every tendon in her neck, to break.

The desk was covered in papers. Deeds, accounts, letters — all of them spread out in a constellation of betrayal that she was mapping with methodical, unhurried precision. I stepped closer, and I could see what she saw: the deed to the farmhouse, transferred to one Josiah Buena's name. The accounts, emptied from Cordelia's inheritance into her husband's coffers. The land — six hundred acres of Blackwood Vale bottomland, the best soil in the county — conveyed in a quitclaim so neat and final it might as well have been a headstone.

In a world where wives could not own property, this was not theft. Not legally. Not in the eyes of any court or clerk or magistrate who would hear the case. It was simply the way things worked — a woman's assets became her husband's upon marriage, as naturally as a river becomes the sea. The law didn't call it theft. The law didn't call it anything at all.
But Cordelia Buena called it theft.
I knew this because she was writing it down.

The scratch of her quill was the only sound in the room — a patient, scratching rhythm, like a creature digging its way out of a coffin. She wrote in a leather-bound ledger, and I leaned close to read over her shoulder, and what I read made my throat close up like a wound.

*taken: the farmhouse & six acres adjacent, by quitclaim deed, April 1793*
*taken: the bottomland, four hundred acres, by conveyance, September 1794*
*taken: the inheritance from my father's estate, the whole of it, by marital right, June 1791*
*taken: the dairy accounts and all profit thereof, by his signature over mine, ongoing*
And then, at the bottom of the page, in letters precise and measured as any lawyer's:

*Total: everything I had.*
*Total owed: everything he took.*

The ledger should have ended there. A woman's account of what was stolen from her, preserved in ink as a record that no court would read and no law would honor. A grief committed to paper because there was nowhere else to put it.
But Cordelia Buena was a Buena, and Buenas do not let things end where the world tells them to end.

In the margin of the ledger, so small it was nearly invisible — a script so minute it might have been an afterthought, a smudge, a strand of ink accidentally dragged — she was writing something else. My eyes adjusted. The letters resolved. And I felt my breath leave my body in a single, shaken exhale.
It was a hex.

A small one, tucked into the margin like a footnote. A curse disguised as a notation. The most Buena thing possible — magic hidden in the mundane, power concealed in the penumbra of a woman's accounting. The hex wasn't targeted at Josiah. It wasn't a curse against the man who'd stolen from her. It was subtler than that, smarter than that. It was a hex against the *deed itself* — a binding woven into the legal language that would, over time, corrode the contract from within. Like rust. Like rot. Like a slow, patient mold that would eat the ink off the page and leave the paper blank and free.
Margin hexes. Curses disguised as footnotes. God, I loved this woman.

I couldn't stand watching her alone. I know — I know it broke the rules of retrocognition. I know I was supposed to be an observer, a witness, a ghost. But the first healing had changed something in me, and I could feel the edges of this memory softening, yielding, becoming a place where I could act. Not change the past — never that. But be *present* in it. Offer what the past had never been offered.
I pulled a chair from against the wall, and it made a soft sound as it dragged across the floorboards.
Cordelia went still.
Her quill paused, mid-stroke. Her shoulders tightened. She didn't turn — not yet — but I watched her fingers curl around the pen like a woman gripping a knife.
"Josiah?" she said, and her voice was steady, but it was the steadiness of a held breath.
"No," I said. "Not Josiah."
She turned.

I don't know what she expected to see. Another woman, perhaps — come to gawk at the wife who'd been robbed. A servant. A neighbor with hard eyes and harder advice. But whatever she saw when she looked at me, it wasn't any of those things. Her eyes moved over my face, my clothes, my hands — and I let her look. I had nothing to hide. Not from her.
"You're Buena," she said. Not a question.
"I'm Aurelia. The seventh."
Something moved in her face — not surprise, not disbelief. Recognition. The way you recognize a face you've seen only in dreams, or in the geometry of your own features reflected in dark water.
"The seventh," she repeated. "How far?"
"Two hundred and twenty-eight years."
She closed her eyes. Opened them. The amber light shifted on her desk, and for a moment, the papers there looked like they were burning.
"Then it's still happening," she said. "The taking. The theft."
"It's still happening."

She set her quill down. She looked at her ledger — the careful accounting, the margin hexes, the whole meticulous record of her erasure — and her face did something terrible. It flickered. The control, the composure, the iron discipline that had held her spine straight and her hands steady — it flickered like a candle in a draft, and beneath it was a grief so vast and old it made the room feel smaller.

"I can't fight this," she said. "A woman can't testify against her husband. A woman can't own property. A woman can't—" Her voice caught. She pressed her lips together and steadied herself, and when she spoke again, her voice was almost too quiet to hear. "A woman can't."
"You can curse the contract."

She looked at me. Two centuries between us, between this amber-lit study and the cottage where my body sat waiting, and the recognition that passed between us was older than either century. It was the recognition of one counting woman looking at another. One ledger-keeper seeing her reflection in the eyes of a girl who would inherit her fight.
"You mean the margins," she said. "The little hexes. You saw them."

"I saw them. They're not enough. The margins aren't enough, Cordelia. You know that. A margin hex is a slow poison — it takes decades, centuries, and the contracts can be rewritten, re-copied, reinforced. You need to hex the *language itself*."
"The language of the deeds?"
"The language of the law. The concept of exchange. Hit the *idea* — not the paper."

Her hands were trembling. I watched her press them flat against the ledger, and I watched her stop them, and I watched her make them still through sheer, impossible force of will. This was a woman who had been eroded to her bedrock and found that the bedrock was granite.
"For you," she said. "I'll write it for you."
She picked up her quill. She opened the ledger to a fresh page — not a margin, not a footnote, but the center of the record, the heart of the accounting — and she began to write.

The scratch of the quill was louder now, or it seemed louder, deeper, a sound that resonated in my sternum like a bell rung underwater. The amber light intensified, pooling around the ledger as if the words themselves were generating heat. Cordelia wrote, and the ink gleamed wet and dark, and the air in the room shifted — thickened — took on a charge like the atmosphere before a storm.
She wrote:
*What was taken in deceit returns sevenfold. Let every contract built on theft corrode from within. Let every deed drawn in bad faith find its ink turned to ash.*
And then, below it, the binding:

*Let no exchange founded on lies stand. Let no transaction made in betrayal hold. What was stolen shall be weight upon the thief — seven times the value, seven times the cost, seven times the burden of the taking. And what was given freely shall compound — seven times the blessing, seven times the return, seven times the light of generosity. This is the ledger's law. This is the accounting. What you take in shadow, you repay in fire. What you give in sun, you receive in gold.*

She finished. She blew gently across the page, and the ink dried instantly — faster than ink should dry, faster than physics allowed, as if the words themselves were eager to exist. The quill was warm in her hand. I could smell it: the hot metal smell of magic, of intent made manifest, of a woman pouring two hundred years of rage and grief and careful, careful counting into a single page.
She set the quill down. She looked at me.

Her face held no satisfaction. This was not a woman rejoicing in her power or gloating over her enemy. This was a woman who had just done the only thing left to do, and the thing she'd done was not enough to save herself. It was never going to be enough to save herself.
It was relief. Pure, aching relief. The relief of setting down a burden you've carried so long you forgot you were carrying it.

"I couldn't stop the theft," she said. "He took it all. The land, the money, the house — he took the house I grew up in, Aurelia. My father built that house. My mother's roses are in that garden. And I can't fight it. No lawyer will take my case. No court will hear my complaint. I am, by law, a thing that belongs to someone else. I couldn't stop him."
"But you could poison the thief's legacy," I said.
She almost smiled. It was a smile like a hairline fracture — fine and almost invisible, but running all the way through. "That's something."
"It's more than something."

"Is it?" She turned to face me fully, and her eyes were wet, and she wasn't blinking, and I realized she was asking me a real question. A question she needed answered by someone who lived in a world she would never see. "Tell me it's enough."
"It's enough." My voice cracked, and I didn't try to hide it. "Cordelia. It's more than enough."

She nodded. Once. A single, decisive movement, like the closing of a ledger. Then she looked down at the page she'd written, and she placed her hand flat on the words, and the glow that rose from the paper was not amber but gold — deep, warm, the color of honey held up to the sun. It climbed her fingers, her wrist, her arm. It filled her like light fills a window.
The prosperity hexes unlocked.

Not the basic version — I'd already touched those, the simple bindings of fairness and exchange. This was the deep version. The root. The original magic that the Buena women had carried across oceans and centuries, the power to bless or curse the very *concept* of exchange itself. To make theft cost sevenfold. To make generosity compound like interest, growing and growing until it became a force that could reshape a town, a valley, a world.

It poured into me through the connection between us — two Buenas, two centuries, one unbroken line of women who counted and kept and cursed in the margins. I felt the hexes settle into my bones like waterlevel in a well, finding their natural depth. They were mine now. Ours. Every Buena who'd ever been robbed, every woman who'd ever been erased, every ledger-keeper who'd ever written the truth in a language no court would read — they were all there, in the hex, in the deep magic of exchange made just.

Cordelia's form was beginning to lighten. The amber glow that surrounded her was lifting her, drawing her somewhere else — somewhere I couldn't follow. Her edges were softening. Her face was peaceful. Not happy — I wouldn't say happy. But at rest.
"For the seventh," she said. Her voice was distant now, the voice of a memory finishing itself. "For the ledger. For the women who count."
And then she was gone.

The farmhouse flickered. The beeswax smell faded. The amber light dimmed and contracted and pulled itself back like a tide retreating from shore, and I was falling again — but gently this time, not the violent ejection of the first retrocognition but a slow, deliberate return, like walking down a staircase you've finally memorized.
I opened my eyes in the cottage.

It felt different. Bigger, somehow, as if the walls had moved outward while I was gone, making room for something. The air was warmer. The shadows in the corners were smaller. The cottage had held things for two hundred years — grief and waiting and the residue of women who couldn't fight — and maybe, now, it was letting some of that go.
I sat at my own desk. I opened my journal — my own ledger, though I hadn't thought of it that way before. And I wrote:

*Cordelia didn't fight for herself. She was too late for herself. Her husband had already taken everything — the law gave him the right, and the world gave him the power, and there was no court and no counsel and no voice that could have stopped him. She couldn't save the farmhouse. She couldn't save the land. She couldn't save her own name.*
*But she wrote a hex into the future for me.*

*Two hundred years ago, a woman sat at a desk in a house that should have been hers, and she poisoned a contract she'd never see enforced, for a girl she'd never meet. She wrote it in the margins first — the only space she had — and then she wrote it in the center of the page, in ink that was older than the law that bound her. She looked at every door that was closed to her and she opened one she couldn't walk through, so that someone else could.*
*If that's not love, I don't know what is.*

I set my pen down. The cottage hummed around me — a low, quiet sound, sympathetic and warm. Somewhere in the walls, a pipe settled. Somewhere in the rafters, a beam creaked. The house was alive with the residue of what had just happened, and it was adjusting itself, the way a room adjusts when someone who's been holding their breath finally exhales.

The prosperity hexes sat in my chest like a second heartbeat. Deep magic. Old magic. The kind that doesn't just change a single transaction but changes the rules of exchange itself. I could feel the boundaries of it — the sevenfold return, the corrosion of theft, the compounding of generosity — and I could feel Cordelia there inside the weave, her intent folded into the structure like a letter sealed inside an envelope, addressed to me, written two centuries before I was born.
I wasn't fighting alone.
I never was.
